title: FRMI Research Workup
markdown:
# FRMI — Research Workup

**FRMI ~$5.62 (Jun 9 close) → intraday ~$7.10 on Jun 10 · market cap roughly $3.6–4.5B depending on intraday print · Net read: a pre-revenue, single-catalyst story where the entire equity thesis rests on landing one anchor tenant, with an unresolved control fight capping the multiple.**

## Company & thesis

Fermi Inc. is a development-stage builder of the "Project Matador" advanced-energy and AI-compute campus near Amarillo, Texas — roughly 7,570 acres designed to wrap gas, nuclear, and solar generation around hyperscale data-center blocks, structured as a REIT and public since its October 2025 IPO at $21. It has no revenue today; the asset is optionality on power-plus-compute capacity at a moment when AI data-center demand is straining the grid.

**Bull case:** Fermi sits at the intersection of the two scarcest inputs in AI infrastructure — large-block power and shovel-ready land with permits. It claims a ~6 GW Clean Air Permit, ~2 GW of secured generation, a 99-year Texas Tech ground lease, an NRC license submission, and an equipment pipeline already financed. If a hyperscaler the size of OpenAI signs an anchor lease, the developer re-rates violently because the market is currently pricing deep execution doubt.

**Bear case:** It is pre-revenue, burning capital, and has already watched one tenant LOI and its $150M construction-funding agreement collapse (December 2025). A boardroom war between ousted co-founder Toby Neugebauer (~40% / ~139M shares) and the sitting board overhangs every decision. The ~1.1 GW year-end-2026 first-power target has been withdrawn. Until a binding lease exists, this is a financing-and-governance story, not an operating one.

## Fundamentals snapshot

| Metric | Figure | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Share price | ~$5.62 (Jun 9 close); intraday ~$7.10 Jun 10 | [1,5] |
| 52-week range | ~$4.47 – ~$37 | [3] |
| IPO (Oct 2025) | $21 | [3] |
| Q1 FY26 net loss | $189M (~$0.30/sh diluted) | [2] |
| % of loss non-cash | ~70% (SBC-driven) | [2] |
| Revenue | $0 (pre-revenue) | [2] |
| Total cash & restricted | $243M (vs. $409M at 12/31/25) | [2] |
| Q1 capex (PP&E) | $441M | [2] |
| Cumulative Matador investment | >$1.4B | [2] |
| Generation secured / permitted | ~2 GW secured · ~6 GW air permit · ~11 GW planned | [2,4] |

## Capital structure & financing

Fermi added roughly **$785M of equipment-financing facilities** in Q1 (including **$500M from MUFG**) plus **$156M from Yorkville** for corporate purposes. Management frames equipment financing as covering most turbine/electrical spend, with broader capital deployment explicitly **matched to tenant agreements**. The cost-of-capital question is the crux: a pre-revenue developer funding a multi-GW campus depends on non-recourse project finance, which is gated by a signed lease. Cash fell to $243M against heavy capex — runway is adequate only if construction stays throttled to tenant progress.

## Ownership & control

The defining feature is the governance contest. **Toby Neugebauer**, terminated for cause April 17, 2026 and the largest shareholder (~139M shares, ~40% with affiliates), is running a proxy campaign for a dual-track strategic process including a possible sale. A shareholder-called **special meeting is anticipated on or around June 30, 2026**. The sitting board, backed by Caddis Capital (~9.3%), is resisting, characterizing the push as an attempt to force a sale at a depressed valuation. This is the single largest reason the equity trades at a discount to its asset narrative.

## Valuation context

For a pre-revenue developer, conventional multiples don't apply; the usable frame is **EV per permitted GW** and market cap versus planned capacity. Against ~6 GW of air-permitted capacity, the current ~$3.6–4.5B enterprise value implies very roughly **$0.6–0.75B per permitted GW** — but "permitted" is not "contracted," and the discount to that figure versus operating power names (VST, CEG, TLN) reflects exactly that gap. These comparisons are directional only: peers have cash-flowing fleets and signed PPAs; Fermi has permits and a pipeline. Treat any implied upside as contingent on tenant conversion, not as a multiple to anchor on.

## Street view

**Citizens (JMP)** carries a **Market Outperform** rating with a **$30 price target**, and on June 10 tied Fermi positively to reports that OpenAI is exploring a ~10 GW Nvidia-backed data-center lease in Ohio — the catalyst behind the ~25% intraday spike. **Evercore** struck a more cautious tone post-Q1, acknowledging the "reset" but awaiting proof before turning bullish. Analyst dispersion is wide and appropriate for a binary, pre-revenue name.

## Risks

1. **No binding lease = no project finance.** The gating catalyst; a prior LOI already collapsed.
2. **Governance instability.** The June 30 special-meeting fight could redirect strategy or force a sale.
3. **Schedule slippage.** The ~1.1 GW year-end-2026 first-power target has been withdrawn.
4. **Liquidity.** $243M cash against heavy capex; financing terms could tighten.
5. **Catalyst is inferential.** The OpenAI read-through is third-party reporting, not a signed deal — vulnerable to giving back gains.
6. **Dilution.** Continued equity/equipment financing pressures the per-share story.

## Sources

1. GuruFocus / Stocktwits — FRMI June 10 surge coverage (OpenAI read-through) · Jun 10, 2026
2. Investing.com / Yahoo Finance — Fermi Q1 2026 results ($189M loss; $243M cash; $785M financing) · May 14, 2026
3. StockAnalysis — FRMI overview (52-week range; IPO $21; proxy filing) · Jun 2026
4. StockTitan — Project Matador milestones (2 GW secured; 6 GW air permit; Texas Tech lease; MUFG) · Jun 2026
5. CNN Markets / Google Finance — FRMI price data (Jun 9 close $5.62; Jun 10 range) · Jun 9–10, 2026
6. StockTitan — Neugebauer proxy fight, special meeting ~Jun 30, 2026 · May–Jun 2026

*Not investment advice.*
